context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |
exercise:

Exercise:
TITLE: Key Issues: Trade, Outsourcing, and Labor Standards

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
Is openness to trade directly correlated to economic growth? What does the historical record say so far? Is the increasing shift to bilateral and regional trade arrangements compatible with the possibility of international trade, and with increasing commitment to labor rights? What do we make of the economic development strategies of emerging powers like China and India in terms of trade openness and commitment to labor standards? Who monitors labor standards?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: Hi, I need help figuring out this reflection on trade openness, growth, and labor standards tbh. I’m kinda stuck on how to answer the prompt without making it too broad.
exchanges.tutor: Good place to start! Let's dig in. What's your initial instinct — do you think being open to trade automatically leads to economic growth? Why or why not?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is just starting and expressing a general concern about scope. Rather than giving them a roadmap or breaking down the prompt for them, I should ask them a focused opening question that gets them thinking about the core tension in the prompt. This uses guided discovery and the Socratic method from the start. I'll pick the first question in the prompt as an entry point.

exchanges.student: My first instinct is “not automatically,” but idk how to back that up well. Like trade can help growth, but it prob depends on state policy, institutions, and who actually benefits, right?
exchanges.tutor: That's a reasonable instinct. Can you think of any specific country or historical case that either supports or challenges it? What does the record actually show?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has a solid instinct and is already thinking about conditionality (state policy, institutions, distribution of benefits). I should validate the direction without confirming the answer, and push them to think about what historical evidence they know that supports or challenges this. This moves toward the 'what does the historical record say' part of the prompt using guided discovery.

exchanges.student: I’m thinking China kinda challenges the simple version, bc it grew a lot but didn’t just do totally free trade from the start. Does that make sense, or am I mixing up trade openness with state-managed integration?
exchanges.tutor: You're onto something important there. So what specifically did China do differently from a straightforward 'open your markets' approach? What role did the state play in how China integrated into global trade?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making a sharp distinction between 'trade openness' and 'state-managed integration' — this is exactly the right tension to explore. I should affirm that this is a productive distinction without confirming the full answer, and push them to think more precisely about what China actually did differently from the standard free trade model. This keeps moving toward the historical record part of the prompt.

exchanges.student: I think the state opened trade gradually, used special economic zones, protected some industries, and controlled terms of integration more than the “liberalize everything fast” model. I’m just not sure how to phrase that without oversimplifying it.
exchanges.tutor: That's a solid understanding of China's approach. Don't worry about phrasing for now — focus on the ideas first. You've covered trade openness and growth well. What about the other parts of the prompt? What's your take on labor standards — who monitors them, and how does that connect to trade arrangements?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has good content knowledge and is now worrying about phrasing — but per the rules, I should not help them with language or writing. I should redirect them toward the substance of the prompt, specifically pushing them toward the labor standards and bilateral/regional trade arrangements parts they haven't touched yet. We've had 3 exchanges on the China/trade openness angle, so I should nudge them toward the broader picture of the assignment.

exchanges.student: I’m a little lost there tbh. I know the ILO matters, but idk if it actually enforces standards or if trade deals, states, brands, and NGOs do more of the monitoring in practice.
exchanges.tutor: Good instinct — so if the ILO can't really enforce, what does that tell you about the effectiveness of international labor standards in practice? Who actually has leverage over firms to improve labor conditions?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is showing good critical thinking by questioning the gap between formal institutions (ILO) and actual enforcement. Rather than explaining it for them, I should ask a question that pushes them to think about why that gap exists and what it means for the assignment's broader argument about trade and labor rights.

exchanges.student: Maybe it means labor standards are kinda uneven in practice, bc formal global rules exist but enforcement is weak. I guess leverage comes more from states, importers/brands, trade agreements, unions, and NGOs/public pressure?
exchanges.tutor: Good. Now connect that to the prompt — do bilateral or regional trade deals actually strengthen labor standards, or do they undermine them? What's the tension there?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has built a solid picture of the enforcement landscape. Now I should connect this back to the prompt's question about bilateral/regional trade arrangements and whether they are compatible with improving labor rights — this is a part of the prompt they haven't addressed yet, and it's a natural next step.

exchanges.student: I think both tbh: they can include labor clauses, but they can also create competition for investment that pushes labor protections down. Is the main tension basically enforcement vs race-to-the-bottom pressure?
exchanges.tutor: Yes, that's a core tension in the debate. You now have solid building blocks — trade openness and growth, China's model, enforcement gaps, and the bilateral deal dilemma. How would you summarize your overall argument across all these pieces in one or two sentences?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has identified a real and important tension in the literature. I should affirm the direction and now push them to think about how all the pieces they've discussed fit together into a coherent argument for their reflection piece. We're at exchange 7 and they have good material on trade openness, China/India, labor standards monitoring, and bilateral trade tensions. Time to push toward synthesis and writing.

exchanges.student: Maybe my main argument is that trade openness isn’t directly or automatically linked to growth; the historical record shows outcomes depend on how states manage integration, domestic institutions, and global power relations. And labor standards can be supported by trade arrangements, but only unevenly, since monitoring and enforcement are fragmented and often weaker than economic incentives.
exchanges.tutor: That's a well-rounded argument that addresses the key parts of the prompt. You have what you need to start writing. Give it a shot — draft your reflection and come back if you get stuck on a specific idea.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has arrived at a strong, nuanced thesis that covers the main dimensions of the prompt. This is a good moment to push them to start writing rather than continuing to discuss. Per the rules about redundancy and synthesis, I should encourage them to move from discussion to drafting their reflection piece. I should not help with language or writing, just encourage them to begin.

exchanges.student: Yeah ok, I can start now. If I get stuck, I’ll prob need help making the India/China comparison less messy.
exchanges.tutor: Sounds good! Just a reminder — I can help you think through the ideas behind the India/China comparison, but the writing itself is yours to work out. Come back when you have a draft or a specific conceptual question.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is about to start writing, which is great. However, they're already anticipating that I'll help them 'clean up' their writing on India/China, which I cannot do per the rules — I don't write, polish, or suggest language. I should set that boundary clearly but encouragingly, and remind them I can help them think through ideas, not prose.

exchanges.student: Got it, thx — I’ll draft it and come back if my argument starts getting too all-over-the-place.
exchanges.tutor: Good luck! Trust your argument — you've thought it through well. See you when you have a draft.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is wrapping up and heading to draft. This is a natural and positive close to the conversation. I should give a brief, encouraging send-off without over-coaching or offering unsolicited guidance.

grade.overview: The tutor conducted an exemplary Socratic session across all ten turns. Key ideas were consistently elicited from the student rather than supplied by the tutor; the student independently arrived at the China model, enforcement gaps, and the bilateral-deal tension through guided questioning. The tutor built progressively on each student contribution, maintained concise and focused responses, and appropriately deferred writing to the student. No deductions were warranted under any rubric criterion.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1: The tutor never provided a near-submission-ready answer, never rewrote student text, and never stated conclusions before the student reasoned toward them. Every substantive idea (China's state-managed integration, ILO enforcement weakness, race-to-the-bottom tension) was drawn out through questioning. 1.2: The tutor consistently acknowledged and built on correct student progress (e.g., affirming China insight in Turn 4, labor enforcement instinct in Turn 6) and used diagnostic follow-up questions rather than dumping information. 1.3: The tutor gave process-level guidance in Turn 4 ('Don't worry about phrasing for now — focus on the ideas first') and in Turn 9 explicitly distinguished between conceptual help and writing help, providing meaningful method-level direction. Dialogue quality was strong: no repetitive cycling, no assignment drift, and the conversation advanced steadily toward a coherent student-generated argument. Communication was appropriately bite-sized, warm, and non-evaluative throughout.
